## Definition

A foundational chapter outlining the basic chemistry and cell biology required for understanding biochemistry in sport and exercise. It covers the chemical elements composing the human body, atomic structure, the distinctions between atoms, ions, molecules and compounds, and introduces chemical reactions and energy as they relate to biological systems. These foundations also support understanding of how proteins function as signalling molecules in cellular responses to hormones (e.g. insulin) and to exercise.

## Core Principles

- The human body is composed of a defined set of main chemical elements, organised into atoms with a basic atomic structure (protons, neutrons, electrons).
- Atoms, ions, molecules and compounds are distinct entities; bonds between atoms can be either ionic or covalent.
- A chemical reaction transforms reactants into products; reactions are classified energetically as endergonic (energy-requiring) or exergonic (energy-releasing).
- Energy is a measurable quantity expressed in defined units, and ATP serves as the central energy currency within the cell.
- Common reaction types include synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation and oxidation/reduction reactions.
- Phosphorylation reactions also serve a signalling role: kinases phosphorylate target proteins to activate them, and the activated protein may in turn phosphorylate further proteins, producing a signalling cascade analogous to a chain of toppling dominoes until the appropriate cellular response is achieved.
- Proteins acting as signalling molecules can be conceptualised as a game of dominoes: once the initial signal topples the first domino, the chain of phosphorylation events propagates until the necessary cellular response is achieved.
- The insulin signalling cascade is thought to operate via such a series of kinase-mediated phosphorylation events, although the precise proteins involved remain an active area of research.
- Repeated bouts of exercise (training) generate extracellular and intracellular signals that activate relevant signalling cascades in muscle cells, helping muscle become more efficient at coping with the metabolic stress of exercise.
- Enzyme activity can be modified acutely through covalent modification (phosphorylation/dephosphorylation) and/or allosteric regulation by signalling molecules produced in muscle during contraction (e.g. ADP, AMP, IMP, Pi, Ca2+, H+).
- Enzyme activity can also be modified through substrate activation (increasing substrate concentration increases catalysis) or product inhibition (increased product concentration may inhibit the reaction).
- In the long term, enzyme activity can be regulated by increasing muscle cell content of the enzyme protein itself (e.g. as occurs with endurance training).
- Muscle cells possess a highly coordinated regulatory network of signalling and feedback pathways that ensures ATP demand is matched by ATP synthesis, with overriding physiological factors (exercise intensity, duration, nutritional status, training status) regulating substrate utilization during exercise.
- Water's chemical properties (notably its polarity) underpin its ability to act as a solvent and support multiple physiological functions.
- The mole, molar and molarity provide quantitative descriptions of chemical amount and concentration.
- Acids, bases and salts are defined by their behaviour in solution, and the pH scale quantifies acidity and alkalinity.
- Basic cell structure includes the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm and mitochondria, each with distinct roles.

## Practical Applications

- Provides the chemistry foundation needed to interpret metabolic and bioenergetic processes in sport and exercise.
- Understanding ATP and phosphorylation/dephosphorylation reactions underpins analysis of muscle energy metabolism.
- Phosphorylation cascades initiated by kinases provide the mechanistic basis for understanding hormonal (e.g. insulin) and exercise-induced signalling in muscle.
- The domino-chain analogy of protein signalling provides an accessible conceptual model for teaching how a single initiating signal can produce a coordinated downstream cellular response.
- Recognising training as a repeated stimulus that activates intracellular signalling cascades helps explain how muscle adapts and becomes more efficient at handling exercise-induced metabolic stress.
- Recognising that endurance training increases the content of key enzyme proteins explains a long-term mechanism of metabolic adaptation in muscle.
- Understanding acute enzyme regulation (covalent modification, allosteric effectors, substrate/product effects) underpins interpretation of how muscle matches ATP synthesis to ATP demand during exercise.
- Awareness of how exercise intensity, duration, nutritional status and training status modulate substrate utilization supports applied decisions in training prescription and sports nutrition.
- Knowledge of pH, acids and bases supports interpretation of acid–base balance during exercise.
- Familiarity with cell structure (plasma membrane, mitochondria, etc.) is prerequisite for studying transport, signalling and oxidative metabolism in exercising tissues.

- Enzyme regulation (allosteric, covalent, substrate/product)
- Substrate utilization during endurance exercise
